card.title: Are Emojis a New Language?
card.teaser: Over 10 billion emojis are sent daily. Linguists say they're not a language — but they're doing something new.
card.coverImagePrompt: A glowing smartphone screen cast against a dark room, streams of emoji spilling outward like sparks, reflecting in a person's eye, cinematic close-up

lessons.title: 10 billion a day
lessons.body: Every day, people send more than 10 billion emojis. The crying-laughing face 😂 was Oxford's 'word' of the year in 2015 — and dictionaries have been debating how to handle emojis ever since. If this many people use something this consistently, isn't that a language? Most linguists would say: almost, but no.

visual.imagePrompt: A massive wall of chat bubbles in a night-lit city, each bubble filled with a single floating emoji, neon palette

lessons.title: What makes something a language
lessons.body: Linguists look for a few features: a productive grammar (rules that can generate infinite new sentences), stable meaning across speakers, and the ability to discuss abstract, unseen things. Emojis fail all three. The meaning of 🍆 depends heavily on context. There's no rule that lets you string emojis into novel 'sentences' the way words work.

keyTerms.definition: The system of rules that lets speakers build unlimited new meaningful sentences
The study of meaning in language
How context shapes meaning, like tone or sarcasm

visual.imagePrompt: A linguistics textbook lying open on a cafe table, a latte beside it, annotations in colored pen across the margins

lessons.title: What emojis actually do
lessons.body: Instead of being a language, emojis work more like tone of voice or gesture. In speech, a raised eyebrow or a smirk carries as much meaning as the words. Typed text has always struggled to transmit that. Emojis plug the gap — 'I'm coming 🙃' feels different from 'I'm coming 😊' even though the words are identical. Linguists call this 'paralanguage'.

visual.imagePrompt: Split illustration: on one side, a medieval scribe writing text in quill; on the other, a modern phone screen showing text plus emojis, thread linking them

lessons.title: Stable, sort of
lessons.body: Studies have shown emoji meaning varies surprisingly widely. One 2016 project analyzed how people interpreted the same emoji across phones — the grinning face 😬, for example, was read as happy on some devices and anguished on others. Meanwhile emoji 'grammar' is still fragile: a string of emojis might tell a story, but only loosely, and only in certain cultures.
lessons.funFact: The oldest known smiley-face :-) in print appeared in 1982, typed by computer scientist Scott Fahlman to help colleagues spot jokes on a Carnegie Mellon message board.

visual.imagePrompt: A row of the same emoji rendered in different corporate styles — Apple, Google, Samsung — each looking subtly different, sterile studio lighting

lessons.title: Not a language — a layer
lessons.body: Emojis are one of the biggest innovations in written communication since punctuation. They're not replacing words; they're doing the work words were never designed to do. Calling them a 'new language' oversells what they are, but undersells what they've done. They've made text, at last, a little more human.

quiz.question: Why do most linguists say emojis aren't a true language?
options: Because they lack a productive grammar and stable shared meaning
quiz.explanation: Languages require grammatical rules and stable meanings, which emojis largely don't have on their own.

quiz.question: What role do emojis play in text communication?
options: They provide tone and nonverbal cues — paralanguage
quiz.explanation: Emojis mimic gestures, facial expressions, and tone — the nonverbal layer speech has but writing lacks.

quiz.question: Why can the 'same' emoji mean different things on different phones?
options: Each vendor renders the same Unicode symbol with a slightly different image
quiz.explanation: Unicode defines the code point, but Apple, Google, and others design their own artwork — which can shift interpretation.

sources.title: Variation in emoji interpretation across platforms
Because Internet: Understanding the New Rules of Language
